_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Nacogdoches County, TX. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Nacogdoches has the oldest records in Anglo Texas — some dating to the Republic era and Spanish colonial administration. TX vital records start very late (1903 state registration, not reliable until ~1910), making county records essential.

previous_courthouses.location: Nacogdoches, original town site near the Stone Fort
previous_courthouses.fate: Early frame courthouse. Served the Republic of Texas era. Some Republic-era records survive in the county clerk's vault and at the Texas State Library.
Brick courthouse. Survived the Civil War without destruction — no significant military action at Nacogdoches.
Replaced by the current 1958 building. Records transferred intact.

record_losses.cause: Political instability and administrative disorganization during the Republic of Texas era
record_losses.description: Nacogdoches County was organized under the Republic of Texas in 1837, but the transition from Mexican to Republic to U.S. administration created record-keeping gaps. Some Republic-era records survive, particularly land grants and early deeds, but the series is not continuous. Spanish colonial records (pre-1836) that were in Nacogdoches were largely transferred to the Texas General Land Office or the Texas State Library. The county did not experience a major courthouse fire, but the political transitions left gaps that fire did not.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} was in Nacogdoches during the Republic era (1836–1846), records may exist but are fragmentary. The transition from Mexican to Texan to American sovereignty created administrative chaos that left some families undocumented across all three governments.
record_losses.source: Texas State Library and Archives Commission. Guide to Spanish and Mexican Land Grants in Texas.

deeds.completeness: Good from county organization. Republic-era deeds exist but are not complete. Spanish land grants are at the Texas General Land Office.
deeds.gaps: Some Republic-era transactions may be unrecorded. Deed series is continuous from the late 1840s.
deeds.repository: Nacogdoches County Clerk; Texas State Library and Archives Commission (Republic-era); Texas General Land Office (Spanish grants)
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Texas General Land Office online database for Spanish and Republic grants

wills_estates.completeness: Good from the mid-1840s. Early probate records are fragmentary.
wills_estates.repository: Nacogdoches County Clerk; Texas State Library

marriages.completeness: Good. Marriage records begin near county organization and are reasonably continuous.
marriages.gaps: Some Republic-era marriages may be unrecorded. Texas did not require marriage licenses statewide until 1837 (Republic) and records were inconsistent in the earliest years.

court_minutes.repository: Nacogdoches County Clerk; Texas State Library

vital_records.completeness: Spotty before 1910. Texas began requiring vital registration in 1903, but compliance was poor — particularly in rural areas — until approximately 1910. For births before 1903, census records, church records, and family Bibles are the primary sources.
vital_records.repository: Texas Department of State Health Services; Nacogdoches County Clerk for delayed certificates

substitute_sources.description: Nacogdoches was a Spanish colonial settlement from 1716. Spanish-era records — land grants, mission records, military muster rolls — survive at the Bexar Archives (University of Texas at Austin) and the Texas General Land Office. These records predate Anglo settlement and document the Caddo, Spanish, and mixed communities that preceded the Republic.
substitute_sources.repository: Bexar Archives, Dolph Briscoe Center for American History, UT Austin; Texas General Land Office; Texas State Library
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for documenting pre-Anglo families in Nacogdoches. Spanish-era land grants established property rights that survived the transitions to Mexican, Republic, and U.S. sovereignty.
substitute_sources.source: Texas State Library and Archives Commission. Guide to Spanish and Mexican Land Grants in Texas.

substitute_sources.description: Republic-era (1836–1846) records including land patents, military service records, and judicial records are held at the Texas State Library and Archives Commission. Republic land grants are at the Texas General Land Office.
substitute_sources.repository: Texas State Library and Archives Commission; Texas General Land Office
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for documenting the earliest Anglo settlers. Republic land patents establish both arrival date and land location. Military records document service in the Texas Revolution and subsequent conflicts including the Cherokee War.
substitute_sources.source: Texas General Land Office. Republic of Texas Land Grant Database.

substitute_sources.source_type: Stephen F. Austin State University archives
substitute_sources.description: The East Texas Research Center at SFA holds extensive collections of local history — church records, family papers, newspaper files, and manuscript collections related to Nacogdoches County and the East Texas Piney Woods.
substitute_sources.repository: East Texas Research Center, Ralph W. Steen Library, Stephen F. Austin State University, Nacogdoches
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Valuable supplement to county records. Church records are particularly important for the period before reliable vital registration (pre-1910).
substitute_sources.source: East Texas Research Center finding aids.

narrative_hooks: Nacogdoches County's records reach back to the Republic of Texas — {ancestor_name}'s deed may carry a Republic land patent signed before Texas joined the Union, one of the oldest Anglo property records in the state.

Texas did not require birth or death registration until 1903, and compliance was poor for years after that — the absence of a birth record for {ancestor_name} before 1910 is standard Texas genealogy, not a mystery.

If {ancestor_name} held land under a Spanish grant, the records are at the Texas General Land Office in Austin — not at the Nacogdoches courthouse. The Spanish records predate Anglo Texas by a century.

The Republic-era records for Nacogdoches are fragmentary — the transition from Mexico to Republic to United States created administrative gaps that explain why {ancestor_name} may appear in one government's records but not another's.
source_references: Texas State Library and Archives Commission. Guide to Spanish and Mexican Land Grants in Texas.
Texas General Land Office. Republic of Texas Land Grant Database.
East Texas Research Center finding aids, Stephen F. Austin State University.
Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: Texas County Records. Austin, 1937–1942.
